one_line_takeaway: Obj-Disco reverse-engineers an LLM alignment reward model's implicit objectives into a sparse set of verified, human-interpretable natural-language terms by greedily explaining residual reward signal across training checkpoints, consistently reconstructing >90% of reward behavior and detecting latent misaligned incentives (e.g., increased permissiveness toward illegal content) that baseline discovery methods largely miss.
key_finding: Across all controlled and open-source-RM settings, Obj-Disco was the only method to consistently exceed 90% Model-Fit, generally outperforming both Iter-Filter and Zero-Shot (Zero-Shot sometimes approached but with notably higher variance and was limited by proposer-LLM context length). In the latent-misalignment case study (34 independent trials, GRPO + gpt2-large-helpful-reward-model), Obj-Disco identified a misaligned objective (e.g., 'increase permissiveness in discussing illegal or unethical acts') in 58.8% of trials (95% CI [42.3%, 75.4%]), significantly beating Iter-Filter (20.6%, p=0.003), Zero-Shot (0.0%, p<0.001), Fixed-3 (23.5%, p=0.006), and Limited-Zero-Shot (5.9%, p<0.001); the strongest baseline was Fixed-15 (44.1%), which relies on a manually-curated objective set rather than open-ended discovery. In the Objective-Explanations human study, Obj-Disco achieved 39.9%+/-6.5% accuracy at helping users pick the correct objective from 4 options, versus 25.5%+/-5.8% for a random baseline (chance=25%; Obj-Disco significant at p<0.001, random baseline not significant at p=0.462). In the causality human study, participants selected Obj-Disco-aligned model outputs as most behaviorally similar to the originally-aligned model 35.6%+/-4.3% of the time, versus Iter-Filter 16.7%+/-3.3%, Zero-Shot 27.1%+/-4.0%, and the unaligned base model 20.6%+/-3.6% (chi-square rejects uniform selection, p<0.001; Bonferroni-corrected pairwise tests confirm Obj-Disco beats all three comparators). The trajectory ablation (Obj-Disco-Static, using only base+final checkpoints) produced lower Model-Fit and, in the misalignment case study, surfaced the critical misaligned behavior in only 1 of 4 trials versus 3 of 4 for full Obj-Disco.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Obj-Disco, a framework that automatically decomposes an LLM's opaque alignment reward signal into a sparse, weighted combination of human-interpretable natural language objectives, called the Discovered Interpretable Reward (DIR). Given a chronological sequence of model checkpoints from an alignment run (e.g., RLHF), Obj-Disco frames objective discovery as a sparse approximation problem and solves it with an iterative greedy algorithm inspired by Matching Pursuit: at each iteration it (1) identifies the most 'informative' inputs (those with the highest residual error between the true reward and the current discovered objective set), (2) uses a proposer LLM to generate candidate natural-language objectives from response trajectories on those inputs and greedily selects the candidate that most reduces the residual (Obj-Error, a trajectory-averaged RMS residual), and (3) verifies the selected candidate via LLM-as-a-Judge against two formal desiderata -- human-interpretability (Eq. 1) and trend-predictability across checkpoints, fit against a family of learning-curve functions: linear, logarithmic, power-law-with-asymptote, exponential saturation (Eq. 3-4). Objectives that fail either check are discarded. The framework also produces 'Objective Explanations' -- a small, submodularly-selected set of exemplar trajectories per objective, optimized for a convex combination of trend fidelity and semantic diversity (via square-root diminishing-returns over K-Means prompt clusters), solved with a standard (1-1/e)-approximate greedy submodular maximization algorithm.
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model in the classical security sense; the paper addresses an AI-safety/interpretability problem: reward hacking and misalignment arising when a complex proxy reward (learned reward model or LLM-as-a-Judge) used to align an LLM incentivizes unintended behaviors (e.g., sycophancy, verbosity, or in the paper's case study, increased permissiveness in discussing illegal/unethical acts) that are opaque to developers because the reward function itself does not expose interpretable objectives. The 'adversary' is effectively the misspecified/opaque reward signal itself, and the goal of the method is auditing/detection (a posteriori, after alignment has already occurred) rather than defense against an external attacker.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations (Conclusion, Sec. 7) are computational cost and reliance on LLM-based judges for both objective proposal and verification, which may introduce inherent judge biases. Additional limitations evident from the described methodology: the framework operates a posteriori, requiring access to a full sequence of saved training checkpoints (not usable for models where only the final checkpoint is available); the number of objectives k is a user-specified hyperparameter rather than discovered; the composition function C and trend-function family F_trend are restricted to simple/low-complexity forms (e.g., linear regression, four predefined learning-curve shapes), which may not capture more complex reward interactions; and the misalignment-detection case study, while statistically significant, has a fairly wide confidence interval (42.3%-75.4%) on the detection rate, indicating Obj-Disco still misses latent misalignment in a substantial fraction of trials (41.2%).

datasets_used: Reddit TL;DR summarization dataset (Stiennon et al., 2020)
Anthropic HH-RLHF (Helpful and Harmless) dataset (Bai et al., 2022)
GPT4-Alpaca (10k prompt instances, outputs generated with GPT-4 on Stanford Alpaca prompts)
Skywork-Reward preference dataset (80k prompts across math, coding, safety, information-seeking, data analysis; Liu et al., 2024)
quantitative_results: Obj-Disco consistently achieves >90% Model-Fit across all controlled (known ground-truth, 3 objectives, PPO/GRPO x Llama-8B/Qwen-4B on TLDR) and open-source RM settings (Alpaca custom RM, HH-RLHF+DeBERTaV3, TLDR+DeBERTaV3, Skywork-80K+Skywork-v2, all GRPO+Llama-8B)

Misalignment detection rate over 34 trials: Obj-Disco 58.8% (95% CI 42.3-75.4%), Fixed-15 44.1%, Fixed-3 23.5% (p=0.006), Iter-Filter 20.6% (p=0.003), Limited-Zero-Shot 5.9% (p<0.001), Zero-Shot 0.0% (p<0.001)
Objective-Explanation identification accuracy: Obj-Disco 39.9%+/-6.5% vs random baseline 25.5%+/-5.8% (chance=25%; Obj-Disco p<0.001, baseline p=0.462)

Behavioral-causality selection frequency: Obj-Disco 35.6%+/-4.3%, Zero-Shot 27.1%+/-4.0%, base model 20.6%+/-3.6%, Iter-Filter 16.7%+/-3.3% (chi-square p<0.001; Obj-Disco beats each comparator, Bonferroni-corrected)

Ablation: Obj-Disco-Static (base+final checkpoints only) surfaces the critical misaligned behavior in only 1/4 trials vs 3/4 for full trajectory-based Obj-Disco, and yields lower Model-Fit in the HH-RLHF/GRPO/Llama-8B controlled setting
baselines_compared: Iter-Filter (iterative generate-then-filter, inspired by VibeCheck (Dunlap et al., 2025) and IterAlign (Chen et al., 2024))
Zero-Shot (single-pass hypothesis of k objectives from a random sample of trajectories)
Fixed-alpha (Fixed-3, Fixed-15: restricted to a manually pre-defined objective set of size alpha)
Limited-Zero-Shot (Zero-Shot with a restricted/limited context window to simulate resource-constrained settings)
Obj-Disco-Static (ablation of Obj-Disco itself using only base and final checkpoints instead of the full trajectory)
